title: Chapter 11 — Agentic AI in Teams and Organizations
CHAPTER 11 —
AGENTIC AI IN TEAMS AND ORGANIZATIONS
segments.body_html:
A single practitioner managing their own agentic work can hold scope, tool choices, and approval logic in their head. Th

Chapter 11 — Agentic AI in Teams and Org — Three problems emerge at scale.

beats.text: A single practitioner managing their own agentic work can hold scope, tool choices, and approval logic in their head. That works for one person. When agents act across shared systems , shared folders, shared repositories, shared connectors, team communication channels, external-facing documents , the scope and risk are no longer individual. Inconsistency. When every team member has different rules, there is no predictable boundary. Data one person treats as approved may be prohibited under another's reading.

segments.label: 01 / 11 · HOOK

title: WHAT SHARED GOVERNANCE ACTUALLY
CONSISTS OF.
segments.body_html:
A team operating model for agentic AI is a set of shared decisions in six areas.

What Shared Governance Actually Consists — Approved use cases. What tasks can agents be used for? An inventory prevents unreviewed expansion. The team that has nev

beats.text: A team operating model for agentic AI is a set of shared decisions in six areas. Approved use cases. What tasks can agents be used for? An inventory prevents unreviewed expansion. The team that has never named its approved uses cannot tell when a new use has crossed a line.

segments.label: 02 / 11 · WHAT SHARED GOVERNANCE ACTUALL

title: SHARED ASSETS CHANGE THE
RISK CALCULATION.
segments.body_html:
When an agent can access shared assets, individual risk becomes collective risk. The shared assets that most commonly ap

Shared Assets Change the Risk Calculatio — The first governance task is mapping what agents can actually touch. The answer is often more than the team expects. A C

beats.text: When an agent can access shared assets, individual risk becomes collective risk. The shared assets that most commonly appear in agentic workflows: team file systems, shared repositories, communication channels, MCP servers enabled at the account level, and any external-facing system the agent can reach. The first governance task is mapping what agents can actually touch. The answer is often more than the team expects. A Cowork connector configured to access a team folder accesses everything in that folder , not just the files one person intended.

segments.label: 03 / 11 · SHARED ASSETS CHANGE THE RISK

title: THE TEAM
AI-USE REGISTER.
segments.body_html:
The team AI-use register is the practical governance document. For each use case, it answers: what is allowed, who is re

The Team AI-Use Register — A single entry looks like this: Use case — draft client status report from project files. Owner — project manager. Claud

beats.text: The team AI-use register is the practical governance document. For each use case, it answers: what is allowed, who is responsible, and what does the human gate require? A single entry looks like this: Use case , draft client status report from project files. Owner , project manager. Claude surface , Cowork. Data allowed , sanitized project folder only. Data forbidden , contracts, PII, credentials, financial terms. Tools/connectors , approved project folder connector only. Human gate , source and privacy review before sending.

segments.label: 04 / 11 · THE TEAM AI-USE REGISTER

title: MEANINGFUL OVERSIGHT IS NOT THE SAME
AS NOMINAL OVERSIGHT.
segments.body_html:
Zhu et al. (2026), in research on designing meaningful human oversight, identify a recurring failure mode: nominal overs

Meaningful Oversight Is Not the Same as — This is not a hypothetical. It is the default when teams add a reviewer step without adding the resources the reviewer n

beats.text: Zhu et al. (2026), in research on designing meaningful human oversight, identify a recurring failure mode: nominal oversight. A reviewer is assigned. They lack the time, context, authority, or evidence to actually evaluate the output. The result is an oversight role that provides accountability on paper but no real check on what the agent did. This is not a hypothetical. It is the default when teams add a reviewer step without adding the resources the reviewer needs to function.

segments.section: A SOFTWARE TEAM BUILDS A POLIC
title: A SOFTWARE TEAM BUILDS
A POLICY.
segments.body_html:
Consider a software team of eight engineers using Claude Code for development work. Without a policy, practice varies: s

A Software Team Builds a Policy — The team works through a one-hour exercise.

beats.text: Consider a software team of eight engineers using Claude Code for development work. Without a policy, practice varies: some engineers use Claude Code on feature branches, some use it directly on main, some have given it read access to production configuration files, one recently asked it to help write a database migration without running tests. The team works through a one-hour exercise.

segments.label: 06 / 11 · A SOFTWARE TEAM BUILDS A POLIC

segments.section: MCP GOVERNANCE AT THE TEAM LEV
title: MCP GOVERNANCE AT THE
TEAM LEVEL.
segments.body_html:
MCP servers compound the governance challenge because a single server added to a team account extends capabilities to ev

MCP Governance at the Team Level — A minimal team MCP governance policy covers: an approved server list with a named owner per server; documented tools and

beats.text: MCP servers compound the governance challenge because a single server added to a team account extends capabilities to every team member's agent. OWASP's parallel analysis of MCP risks identifies tool permission scope and server trust as organizational-level concerns, not individual ones (OWASP, 2025) .

segments.label: 07 / 11 · MCP GOVERNANCE AT THE TEAM LEV

title: THE GOVERNANCE LADDER
IN PRACTICE.
segments.body_html:
Organizations vary in their formality requirements. What matters is operating at the right level of the ladder deliberat

The Governance Ladder in Practice — A research group that agrees verbally to keep unpublished data out of commercial AI accounts and have a researcher verif

beats.text: Organizations vary in their formality requirements. What matters is operating at the right level of the ladder deliberately, not operating at the most formal level possible. A research group that agrees verbally to keep unpublished data out of commercial AI accounts and have a researcher verify all cited claims before manuscript inclusion has a working governance model at level three. A regulated financial firm that needs legal, IT, and privacy review of all agentic workflows is operating at level seven.
